questions.question: Explain the concept of write-ahead logging (WAL) protocol in database recovery.
keyPoints: WAL requires log records to be written to stable storage before data modifications
This ensures that log can be used to recover or recreate data changes
If system crashes, log can reconstruct the state of committed transactions
Order: Write log → Write data → Commit

questions.question: What are the different types of failures that can occur in a DBMS? Explain each briefly.
keyPoints: Transaction failure: Error in transaction logic or deadlock
System crash: Loss of volatile memory contents
Disk failure: Physical damage to storage media
Media failure: Natural disasters or complete loss of storage

questions.question: Describe the two main approaches to database recovery with advantages of each.
keyPoints: Shadow paging: Keeps old page copies until commit; simple undo, but poor for concurrent transactions
Log-based recovery: Maintains transaction log; supports concurrent transactions but complex recovery
Comparison: Shadow paging - faster for few updates; Log-based - better for high transaction throughput

questions.question: What is the purpose of checkpoints in log-based recovery? How do they improve recovery performance?
keyPoints: Checkpoint is a synchronization point saving transaction state to log
All dirty pages are flushed to disk at checkpoint
Reduces recovery time by limiting log scan from last checkpoint
Transaction table and dirty page table are saved for quick recovery

questions.question: Explain the difference between redo and undo operations in transaction recovery.
keyPoints: Redo: Reapplies the effects of committed transactions from log
Undo: Reverses effects of uncommitted (aborted) transactions
Redo is needed because committed changes may be only in buffer
Undo is needed to maintain atomicity for failed transactions
Both operations use log records to determine what to restore/reverse

questions.question: Explain the three phases of the ARIES recovery algorithm.
keyPoints: Analysis: Scans log forward to build transaction table and dirty page table
Redo: Reapplies all updates from checkpoint to bring database to current state
Undo: Reverses effects of uncommitted transactions in reverse order
Analysis identifies what needs redo/undo; Redo ensures all committed work is reflected; Undo removes uncommitted work

questions.question: What is the transaction table and dirty page table in ARIES? Explain their roles in recovery.
keyPoints: Transaction table: Tracks all active transactions with status (active/commit/abort) and last LSN
Dirty page table: Lists pages modified in buffer but not written to disk with their recovery LSN
Transaction table helps identify which transactions need undo
Dirty page table helps identify which pages need redo during recovery
Both tables are built during analysis phase and used in redo/undo phases

questions.question: Explain the log-based recovery mechanism in detail. How does it handle transaction commit and abort? Include the role of log records in recovery.
keyPoints: Log maintains record of all database modifications: BEGIN, UPDATE (old/new values), COMMIT, ABORT
Write-ahead logging: Log written before data changes
On commit: Write COMMIT record to log, ensure log flushed to disk
On abort: Read log and perform undo operations in reverse order
Recovery after crash: Scan log from last checkpoint, redo committed transactions, undo uncommitted
Log sequence numbers (LSN) track order of operations
Each update record contains previous LSN for chaining
Undo uses compensation log records (CLRs) to record undone actions for crash safety

questions.question: Describe the shadow paging recovery technique. How does it ensure transaction atomicity? What are its advantages and disadvantages?
keyPoints: Shadow paging maintains two page tables: current and shadow
Updates are made to a shadow copy of pages, original remains unchanged
When transaction commits, shadow page table becomes current
On abort, simply discard shadow copy - original intact
Advantages: Simple undo (no undo needed), no log records needed for undo, fast abort
Disadvantages: Sequential access efficiency, copy-on-write overhead, difficult concurrent transaction support
Atomicity ensured because changes only visible after commit
Durability: Committed page table saved to stable storage

questions.question: Explain the importance of ACID properties in transaction recovery with specific examples of how each property is ensured during recovery.
keyPoints: Atomicity: Either all effects saved (commit) or none (abort) - undo mechanism ensures this
Consistency: Recovery brings database from one consistent state to another
Isolation: Concurrency control ensures serializable execution, recovery doesn't affect isolation
Durability: Once committed, effects persist - redo mechanism ensures committed changes survive
Example of atomicity: If transaction fails mid-execution, all changes rolled back
Example of durability: If crash occurs after commit, redo reapplies committed changes
WAL ensures atomicity and durability during normal operation
Checkpoints ensure consistency points for recovery

questions.question: Explain the media recovery process in DBMS. How does it differ from transaction recovery? Discuss backup and restore procedures.
keyPoints: Media recovery handles disk failure/corruption, not just system crash
Requires physical backup of database files taken periodically
Full backup: Complete copy of all database files
Incremental backup: Only changes since last backup
Recovery procedure: Restore from latest backup, apply transaction logs from backup point
Difference from transaction recovery: Media recovery deals with permanent loss of data on disk
Transaction recovery handles volatile memory loss (system crash)
Media recovery may involve multiple backups and long log application
Media recovery ensures database restored to most recent consistent state before failure
